terms.definition: The city's main bank account that pays for everyday services like police, fire, and parks. It's funded mostly by taxes and is the biggest pot of money the city manages.
terms.example: Cerritos uses its General Fund (about $95 million) to pay for sheriff patrols, park maintenance, and street repairs.

terms.definition: Money set aside for big, one-time projects like building a new park, repaving a road, or upgrading a water treatment plant. These are things that last many years.
terms.example: The city's capital budget might include $2 million to renovate a playground that will serve kids for the next 20 years.

terms.definition: The plan for day-to-day spending that keeps the city running -- salaries, supplies, utilities, and regular maintenance. Think of it like a household's monthly bills.
terms.example: The operating budget covers things like paying firefighters every month and keeping the lights on at City Hall.

terms.definition: All the money coming into the city's budget. This includes taxes, fees, grants, and other income. It's like the city's paycheck.
terms.example: Cerritos collects about $95 million in revenue each year, with property tax and sales tax being the two biggest sources.

terms.definition: Money the city spends on services, projects, and operations. Every dollar going out of the budget is an expenditure.
terms.example: The city's largest expenditure is the sheriff contract at about $30 million per year -- that's roughly one-third of the whole budget.

terms.definition: A Latin phrase meaning 'per person.' It's a way to figure out how much something costs for each resident, which makes it easier to compare cities of different sizes.
terms.example: If Cerritos spends $95 million for 51,460 people, that's about $1,846 per capita -- meaning each resident's share is roughly $1,846.

terms.definition: The 12-month period the city uses for its budget. In California, the fiscal year runs from July 1 to June 30 -- so 'FY 2024-25' means July 2024 through June 2025.
terms.example: Schools start in September, but the city's fiscal year starts in July, so the 2024-25 budget kicks in two months before school starts.

terms.definition: A way for the city to borrow money for big projects by promising to pay it back over time with interest -- kind of like a loan. Voters often have to approve bonds.
terms.example: The city might issue a $10 million bond to build a new community center and pay it back over 20 years using tax revenue.

terms.definition: A separate budget for a city service that charges fees to cover its own costs, like water or sewer. It's meant to be self-supporting, like a mini business within the city.
terms.example: Cerritos runs its water utility as an enterprise fund -- the fees on your water bill pay for water treatment, not tax dollars.

terms.definition: Money that can only be spent on a specific purpose, usually because of a law or grant requirement. The city can't use it for anything else.
terms.example: Gas tax money from the state is restricted -- Cerritos must use it for road repairs and can't spend it on parks or police.

terms.definition: A surplus means the city collected more money than it spent (good news!). A deficit means it spent more than it earned (a warning sign that cuts or new revenue may be needed).
terms.example: If the city collects $96 million in revenue but only spends $95 million, it has a $1 million surplus that can go into savings.

terms.definition: Official permission from the City Council to spend a specific amount of money on something. No city department can spend money without an appropriation.
terms.example: The City Council appropriated $500,000 for new playground equipment -- that's the green light for the Parks Department to make the purchase.

terms.definition: A single, specific entry in the budget that shows exactly what money is being spent on. It's the most detailed level of the budget.
terms.example: Under Parks & Recreation, you might see a line item for 'Tree Planting & Care: $2,050,000' -- that tells you exactly how much goes to trees.

terms.definition: The city's savings account -- money left over from previous years that hasn't been spent. A healthy fund balance helps the city handle emergencies or unexpected costs.
terms.example: Cerritos keeps a fund balance as a rainy-day reserve so it can still pay for services if tax revenue drops unexpectedly.

terms.definition: The total value of all the property, businesses, and economic activity that the city can collect taxes on. A bigger tax base means more potential revenue.
terms.example: The Cerritos Auto Square and Cerritos Towne Center are huge parts of the city's tax base because they generate millions in sales tax.
